title: The Social Security Administration's Hearings Backlog and Average Processing Times
description: SSA Office of the Inspector General, Office of Audit, final report A-05-22-51159 (September 2023) determining whether the Office of Hearing Operations' (OHO) Compassionate And REsponsive Service (CARES) Plan initiatives reduced the hearings backlog and average processing times (APT). Background: SSA issued the CARES Plan in January 2016 to cut the pending hearings backlog toward an APT of 270 days, when over 1 million people awaited hearing decisions and APT was 543 days; the Plan was updated in 2017 (APT peaked at 605 days) and 2019, totaling 45 initiatives. Key finding: while the backlog and APT generally decreased FY2016-FY2022, OIG could not determine whether the CARES initiatives caused the reduction — 42 of 45 initiatives lacked sufficient measurements/metrics and SSA could not document a direct link, because SSA never established formal measurements. Memorandum dated September 7, 2023, to Acting Commissioner Kilolo Kijakazi from IG Gail S. Ennis.
geographic_coverage: United States (federal — SSA Office of Hearing Operations, national)

source.publisher: SSA Office of the Inspector General
source.title: The Social Security Administration's Hearings Backlog and Average Processing Times (A-05-22-51159)

notes: Primary published U.S. government audit report (SSA OIG, Office of Audit), hence Authoritative. Real metadata extracted from pages 1-3 of 30: report number A-05-22-51159, September 2023, CARES Plan objective, memorandum dated 2023-09-07 to Acting Commissioner Kijakazi, and the headline finding (42 of 45 initiatives lacked metrics). No sibling .source.txt; exact source URL unknown, so oig.ssa.gov canonical domain used (deep link not invented).
